coredata.dc:title: Comparison between hydrodynamic and ultrasound cavitation on the inactivation of lipoxygenase and physicochemical properties of soy milk
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Guangjie
Luo, Ji
Li, Fang
Li, Dandan
Han, Yongbin
Tao, Yang
coredata.dc:description: The effects of hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) and ultrasound cavitation (UC) on the lipoxygenase activity and physicochemical properties of soy milk were evaluated. The results revealed that both ultrasound cavitation and hydrodynamic cavitation significantly inactivated the lipoxygenase activity. After the exposure to ultrasound cavitation at 522.5 W/L and 70 °C for 12 min, the lipoxygenase activity was inactivated by 96.47 %. Meanwhile, HC treatment with the cavitation number of 0.0133 for 240 min led to the loss of 79.31 % of lipoxygenase activity. An artificial neural network was used to model and visualize the effects of different parameters after ultrasound cavitation treatment on the inactivation efficiency of soy milk. Turbiscan test results showed that hydrodynamic and ultrasound cavitation decreased the instability index and particle size of soy milk. Moreover, the total free amino acid content was significantly increased after hydrodynamic and ultrasound cavitation treatment. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry showed that the total content of beany flavor compounds decreased after acoustic cavitation and HC treatment. Acoustic cavitation and HC affected the tertiary and secondary structure of soy milk, which was related to the inactivation of lipoxygenase. We aim to explore a potential and effective way of the application in soy milk processing by comparing the ultrasound equipped with heat treatment and hydrodymic cavitation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrodynamic cavitation
Ultrasound cavitation
Lipoxygenase
Soy milk
Artificial neural network

Comparison among thermal treatment, ultrasound cavitation and hydrodynamic cavitation in the inactivation of soy milk lipoxygenase activity
Temperature profile of ultrasound cavitation and hydrodynamic cavitation
Parametric study of ultrasonic inactivation of lipoxygenase in soy milk
Effect of ultrasound and hydrodynamic cavitation on color
Effect of treatments on the total amino acid content
Effect of US and HC on the tertiary and secondary structure of protein in soy milk
Volatile flavor compounds in soy milk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of ultrasound and hydrodynamic cavitationtreatments on lipoxygenase activity of soy milk. Different AED levels at 60 °C (a), 70 °C (b) and 80 °C (c). (d): HC treatment. Note: 0 W/L represents that the acoustic energy density of the sample is zero.

(a): architecture of ANN model to predict lipoxygenase activity as a function of AED level, temperature and time; (b): 4D graph showing the effects of different parameters on the lipoxygenase activity.

Thermal histories of the ultrasound and hydrodynamic cavitationtreatments applied to the soy milk. The red dotted line on the graph represents the moderate heat treatment supplied by the external source.
Effects of ultrasound (a) and hydrodynamic cavitation (b)treatments on changes of TSI on soy milk.
Effects of ultrasound (a) and hydrodynamic cavitation (b)treatments on Particle size distributions of soy milk.
Effects of ultrasound (a) and hydrodynamic cavitation (b)treatments on total free amino acid content of soy milk.
Effects of ultrasound and hydrodynamic cavitationtreatments on tertiary and secondary structure of protein in soy milk.
(a) Heat map visualization, (b) changes in the content (ug/L) of volatile flavor compounds.
Effects of different treatment conditions on pH and color of soy milk samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Different letters represent significant different means among samples in the same line at p < 0.05. HC-1,HC-2 and HC-4 represent HC treatment for 1 h,2h and 4 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Concentrations of volatile compounds in soy milk by ultrasound and HC treatments.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Different letters represent significant different means among samples in the same line at p < 0.05.

head.ce:title: Comparison between hydrodynamic and ultrasound cavitation on the inactivation of lipoxygenase and physicochemical properties of soy milk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing 210095, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Food Science and Technology, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing 210095, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effects of hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) and ultrasound cavitation (UC) on the lipoxygenase activity and physicochemical properties of soy milk were evaluated. The results revealed that both ultrasound cavitation and hydrodynamic cavitation significantly inactivated the lipoxygenase activity. After the exposure to ultrasound cavitation at 522.5 W/L and 70 °C for 12 min, the lipoxygenase activity was inactivated by 96.47 %. Meanwhile, HC treatment with the cavitation number of 0.0133 for 240 min led to the loss of 79.31 % of lipoxygenase activity. An artificial neural network was used to model and visualize the effects of different parameters after ultrasound cavitation treatment on the inactivation efficiency of soy milk. Turbiscan test results showed that hydrodynamic and ultrasound cavitation decreased the instability index and particle size of soy milk. Moreover, the total free amino acid content was significantly increased after hydrodynamic and ultrasound cavitation treatment. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry showed that the total content of beany flavor compounds decreased after acoustic cavitation and HC treatment. Acoustic cavitation and HC affected the tertiary and secondary structure of soy milk, which was related to the inactivation of lipoxygenase. We aim to explore a potential and effective way of the application in soy milk processing by comparing the ultrasound equipped with heat treatment and hydrodymic cavitation.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing awareness of health and sustainability has supported the development of plant protein-based products to reduce the environmental impacts and natural resource depletion associated with meat production[1]. As a major plant-based product, soy milk is an animal milk alternative that contains abundant proteins, vitamins and bioactive compounds, thereby being considered as an alternative to animal milk (Vagadia et al., 2018). Despite that the demand for soy milk is booming, the presence of unpleasant odors can lower consumers’ acceptance of soy milk products [3]. To date, these off-flavors of soy milk have been correlated to the action of lipoxygenase enzymes during soy processing [4]. Generally, lipoxygenase can oxidize polyunsaturated fatty acids and degrade hydroperoxides into various volatile compounds such as hexanal responsible for the off-flavors [2]. Therefore, it is necessary to inactivate lipoxygenase to improve the flavors of soy milk.

A series of recent studies have indicated that both conventional and emerging technologies can be employed to inactivate lipoxygenase in soy products to improve their stability and flavor [4,5,6,7]. Li et al. [4] evaluated the effects of treatment time, pulse strength, pulse frequency and pulse width on the inactivation of lipoxygenase using pulsed electric fields. They found that the maximum inactivation (88 %) of lipoxygenase was achieved at 42 kV/cm for 1036 μs with a 400 Hz of pulse frequency and 2 μs pulse width. Navicha et al. [8] reported that roasting at 100 °C for 80 min inactivated over 90 % of lipoxygenase and reduced the off-odors in soy milk, but an extremely high temperature also caused some negative changes in the yield, solid content, color and nutritional value. Due to the low inactivation efficiency, high energy-consumption, and unexpected damage in nutrients, other emerging technologies should be used to inactivate lipoxygenase and improve the sensory quality of soy milk.

Power ultrasound, is a sustainable and green technology, that has shown potential benefits for the modification of the physicochemical properties and biological activity of soy products [9,10]. Such benefits are mainly contributed by physical and sonochemical effects resulting from the acoustic cavitation[11]. The violent bubble collapse of cavitation bubbles produces strong mechanical effects (i.e. shear forces, micro-jetting and shockwaves) that produce hydrogen and hydroxyl radicals (Tao et al., 2021). For instance, Omura et al. [13] explored the effects of ultrasonic parameters and protein concentration on the techno-functional properties of soy protein isolate. The results indicated that the water and oil holding capacities, foaming properties, emulsifying properties and gelling properties were all improved after sonication at 562 W for 120 s. To further improve the heat-transfer and mass-transfer during food processing, thermosonication can potentially combine the benefits of heat and ultrasound [14]. However, to our knowledge, few study has been used to determine effects of thermosonication on the quality of soy milk. Another technology, Hydrodynamic cavitation (HC), is widely considered to have great potential for industrial scale process intensification [39]. Its principle is related to the cavitation which a localized pressure drop is generated when a moving fluid encounters a sudden change in velocity [16]. Although the methods for hydrodynamic cavitation and ultrasound are different, they generate similar cavitational effects. Compared with ultrasound, hydrodynamic cavitation is easier for industrial scale-up applications [17]. Ren et al. [18] compared the effects of hydrodynamic and ultrasound cavitation on the physicochemical and functional properties of soy protein isolated. In that study, both hydrodynamic cavitation and ultrasound can both reduce the particle size, viscosity and foam stability and increased the surface hydrophobicity, solubility and emulsifying properties of soy protein isolate. Salve et al. [19] reported that hydrodynamic cavitation was superior to ultrasound treatment in terms of improving rheological properties of peanut milk. However, few studies have focussed on the role of hydrodynamic cavitation on the inactivation of lipoxygenase and how it changes the modification of physicochemical properties of soy milk.

Various parameters are involved in the ultrasound and hydrodynamic cavitation-based inactivation of lipoxygenase [12,20]. To analyze these parameters, multivariate analysis is normally required to explore the effect of every single factor, as well as their combined effects on the inactivation progress. Artificial neural networks (ANN) are powerful mathematical methods suitable for modeling and simulation of various processes in real applications.The methodology don t need the explicit expressions of the physical meaning of the system or process under investigation. [21,22]. By using this powerful tool, the effects of all factors individually and in combination can be clearly visualized. ANN has been applied to visualize the effects of different ultrasound parameters on the extraction and adsorption of bioactives[20,23,24]but has not been employed to visualize parameters of acoustic and hydrodynamic cavitation-induced inactivation of lipoxygenase.

In this study, ultrasound cavitation (UC) and hydrodynamic cavitation (HC) were applied to inactivate lipoxygenase and improve the quality of soy milk. ANN was used to model and visualize the effects of various parameters on the UC- and HC-induced inactivation of lipoxygenase. Changes in the physicochemical properties of soy milk (i.e. stability, size distribution, color, free amino acid content, volatile compounds, and conformational changes of proteins) were evaluated during UC and HC treatment. The results provide guidance for producing high-quality soy milk using UC and HC in the food industry.

Dried mature soybeans (cultivar: Hefeng-25) were purchased from Hailun Jiahe Doubang soybean Technology Co., Ltd. Hailun City, China. Then, soybeans with smooth surface, plump grain, and bright color were selected for this investigation. Linoleic acid was bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co.,Ltd., Shanghai, China. All the other reagents were of the analytical level.

Dried soybeans were washed and hydrated in distilled water at a ratio of 1:4 (g: mL) for 16 h at room temperature. Then, excess water was drained and the hydrated soybeans were ground with distilled water at a ratio of 1:8 (g: mL) for 3 min in a high-speed blender (MJ-WBL2501A, Midea Household Appliance Manufacturing Co., LTD. Wuhu, China). After grounding, the suspension was filtered by two layers cheesecloth to get fresh soy milk [55]. Fresh soy milk samples were prepared before each experiment and kept under 4 oC. Raw soymilk samples were treated within 24 h to minimize enzyme activities.

For UC test, an 20-kHz ultrasound processor (Model XSJ-650 W, Xinshangjia Biotechnology Co., LTD. Wuxi, China) with a tip diameter of 8 mm was used. 50 mL of raw soy milk was sonicated in a 100-mL glass beaker. The sample temperature during sonication was controlled by a cooling circulation system (Model DL-1005, Xinshangjia Biotechnology Co., LTD. Wuxi, China). Ultrasound probe was inserted 2 cm below the soy milk surface, and worked in a pulsing mode (3 s on and 3 s off). The actual acoustic energy density (AED) distributed in the soy milk solution corresponding to a certain ultrasonic power was measured by the calorimetry method. Ultrasound power was then calculated using the following equation:
(1)
p = m C p
dT
dt
The acoustic energy density (AED) was then calculated by dividing measured power with the volume of distilled water inside. In this work, three temperature levels (60, 70 and 80 °C) and two acoustic energy density levels (336.8 and 522.5 W/L) were applied for the processing. Samples were taken periodically for the physicochemical analysis.

For HC processing, a self-assembled HC device (10 L) was used for this work. According to Supplementary Fig. S1b, this device was composed by a closed-loop pipe (stainless steel, 25 mm internal diameter), a centrifugal single-stage pump(1.5 kW), a cylindrical tank and cooling jacket. The other components of the system included ball valves, pressure gauges, flanges to hold the orifice plate, flowmeter, and venturi tube (25 mm internal diameter). The schematic diagram of the laboratory setup for hydrodynamic cavitation is shown in Supplementary Fig. S1 . For a single test, about 5 L of soy milk was consumed, and the temperature generated by cavitation was not controlled. The venturi tube-based HC system was run for same intensity of cavitation, and samples were collected at different processing times (1, 2, 3, 4 h). All the samples were instantly stored on ice for further analysis. The density of water was used to calculate the cavitation number before soy milk processing treated with hydrodynamic cavitation, and the cavitation number is defined as:
(2)
C =
p -
p v
ρ
v
0
2
/ 2

where p is the downstream pressure (Pa), pv is the vapor pressure of water (Pa), ρ
is the density of water (kg/m3), v0 is the average velocity near the orifice (m/s), Q is the flow of water (m3/s).

The lipoxygenase enzyme activity assay was carried out following a published method with slight modifications [25]. The collected soy milk sample was first centrifuged at 12,000 rpm and 4 °C for 30 min. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.2-µm filter.

To prepare the substrate solution, 27 µL pure linoleic acid, 25 µL Tween-20, and 8 mL deionized water were mixed. Then, approximately 1.1 mL of 0.5 M NaOH was added slowly to clarify the mixture. Next, distilled water was added to the solution, making the final volume 50 mL. After that, 2.78 mL phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH 8.0), 200 µL substrate solution, and 20 µL soy milk sample diluted properly were mixed and incubated at room temperature for 3 min. The increment in the absorbance at 234 nm was monitored by a spectrometer (UV-5800PC, Shanghai Metash Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai China). The lipoxygenase activity was estimated from the slope of the obtained linear curve. One unit of lipoxygenase activity was defined as a change of 0.001 units of the absorbance per minute and milliliter of enzyme extract at room temperature. The inactivation efficiency (IE) of lipoxygenase was calculated as:
(3)
I E
( % )
= 1 -
A
A 0
× 100 %
where A and A0 are the enzyme activities of treated and untreated samples, respectively.

The particle size distribution of soy milk was determined with a Malvern 3000 (Malvern Instrument Co., Ltd., Malvern, Nottinghamshire, Worcestershire, UK). The obscuration rate, particlerefractive index, and particle absorption rate were set as 5–10 %, 1.57, and 0.001, respectively [28].

A colorimeter (YS3060, Jiulian Technology Co., Ltd., Suzhou, China) was used to determine the color parameters of uncentrifuged soy milk. The L*(brightness/darkness), a* (redness/greenness), and b* (yellowness/blueness) values were obtained according to the Commission International de I’ Eclairage (CIE) [29]. The pH was determined by a pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Shanghai, China). The total color difference (TCD) was obtained from calculating L*, a* and b* values:
(4)
T C D =
L
c
∗
-
L
s
∗
2
+
a
c
∗
-
a
s
∗
2
+
b
c
∗
-
b
s
∗
2

Where
L
c
∗
,
a
c
∗
and
b
c
∗
are the values from the untreated sample.
L
s
∗
,
a
s
∗
and
b
s
∗
are the values from the treated sample.

The stability of the soy milk was monitored by the Turbiscan analysis (Turbiscan Tower, Formulaction, France). The uncentrifuged 20 mL soy milk in the glass cells were scanned for 1 h from the bottom to the top with a pulsed near-infrared light source (λ = 880 nm) and two synchronized detectors [30].

The amino acids in the soy milk samples were determined using a standard Agilent protocol [31]. First, 5 mL of 10 % trichloroacetic acid was added to 5 mL of soy milk to precipitate protein. After 30 min, the soy milk was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.2 µm filler, and used for the measurement of free amino acids. An Agilent RP Poroshell C-18 column (100 * 2.7 mm) was utilized to separate the amino acids. A binary mobile phase (mixture of A and B) was applied at a flow rate of 1.2 mL/min. Mobile phase A included 10 mM sodium borate, 10 mM sodium hydrogen phosphate, and 5 mM sodium azide. Mobile phase B included HPLC/Spectro acetonitrile, HPLC/Spectro methanol, and deionised water at a ratio of 45:45:10 (v/v/v). The gradient elution program was as follows: 0–0.35 min, 2 % B; 0.35–13.4 min, 57 % B; 13.4–13.5 min, 100 % B; 13.5–15.7 min, 100 % B; 15.7–15.8 min, 2 % B; 15.8–18 min, 2 % B. The content was expressed as μg /mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of volatile flavor compounds in soy milk
ce:section.ce:para: The headspace-solid phase microextraction-gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (HS-SPME-GC–MS) was utilized to analyze the volatile compounds of soy milk according to a previous method with slight modifications [32]. The soy milk sample (20.0 mL) was placed in a flask mixing 2-methyl-3-heptanone (24.50 μL, 816 μg/mL internal standard) and equilibrated at 45 °C for 25 min. After equilibration, the SPME fiber (50/30 μm DVB/CAR/PDMS, Stableflex, Supelco Co., Ltd., Bellefonte, Pennsylvania, USA) was inserted into the headspace vial at 50 °C for 30 min to absorb the volatiles. Then, the SPME fiber containing the extracts was rapidly transferred to the injector port for 5-min desorption at 250 °C. The gas chromatograph (TRACE 1310, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd., Waltham, Massachusetts, USA) coupled with a triple quadrupole mass spectrometer ((TSQ 9000, Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd., Waltham, Massachusetts, USA) was utilized for analysis. A DB-WAX capillary column (30 m × 0.32 mm × 0.25 μm) was used to separate volatile compounds. All injections were performed in spitless mode. The flow rate of helium (99.999 %) as carrier gas was 1.2 mL/min. The operating programs were as follows: the temperature of the injection port was set as 250 °C. The initial oven temperature was maintained at 40 ◦C for 2 min, raised rapidly at 5 °C / min to 220 °C, then held for 2 min. The GC − MS transfer line was set at 280 °C; an electron-impact (EI) mode was generated at 70 eV; a scan range of 40–550 m/z was sued; and an ion source temperature of 300 °C. electron impact mass spectra were at 70 eV, and a scan range of m/z 38–––400 was used. All compounds were identified according to the NIST 2017 mass spectral library. Flavor compounds with matching score > 80 was selected as the possible component. The linear retention index (LRI) of each compound was calculated according to the retention times of C5–C30 n-alkanes under the same conditions.

The secondary structure of soy milk was determined by J-815CD spectroscopy (Model JASCO J-1500, JASCO Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). All the samples before and after treatment were centrifuged at 10,000 r/min to remove insoluble residues, meanwhile the supernatant was diluted a certain multiple. The CD spectrum was recorded using a CD spectrometer in a 0.1 cm quartz cuvette at room temperature, and the scanning range was 190–250 nm with a 0.5 nm scanning step and a 1 nm bandwidth, respectively. The scanning speed was 50 nm/min. The percentage of α-helix, β-sheet, β-turn and random coils of the samples was calculated on the Bestsel Website [33].

The Intrinsic fluorescence emission spectrum of soy milk was determined by a Card-F980 fluorescence spectrometer (Lengguang, Shanghai, China) to characterize the changes of the tertiary structure of the soy milk [34]. All the samples were diluted 300 times with deionized water, and the experiment was carried out using a quartz cuvette with a 200 μL capacity. The excitation wavelength was 280 nm, emission spectrum was 300–450 nm, and data interval was 1 nm. The scanning speed was 600 nm/min, and the bandwidth of excitation and emission was 5 nm. The intensity of the fluorescence was measured in an arbitrary unit (A.U.).

A four-layered feed-forward artificial neural network was constructed to combine the operating parameters for parametric study, namely the AED level, temperature, and processing time with the inactivation efficiency. The input layer had four nodes representing the AED level, temperature and processing time. The output layer had one neuron denoting the inactivation efficiency. In the aforementioned network, the linear transfer function (purelin), log-sigmoid transfer function (logsig) and hyperbolic tangent sigmoid transfer function (tansig) were considered. The predictive ability of the constructed ANN model for both training and cross-validation datasets was evaluated using three statistical indicators, including the coefficient of determination (R2 ) and absolute average deviation (AAD):
(5)
R 2
= 1 -
∑
i = 1
n
Y
i , p
-
Y
i , e
2
∑
i = 1
n
Y
i , e
-
Y m
2
(6)
AAD =
∑
i = 1
n
Y
i , p
-
Y
i , e
/
Y
i , e
n
× 100

The neural network toolbox in MATLAB r2016a was used for construction of the artificial neural network model. Details about ANN modeling can be found in our previous works [26]. Once ANN model was built, a 4D graph reflecting the influences of the AED level, temperature and processing time on the inactivation efficiency was generated using the “slice” function in MATLAB [27]. The code to solve the “slice” function about a 4D graph is listed in Supplementary Text.

IBM SPSS Statistics 25 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was used for statistical analysis. The data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD) of triplicates. Differences among groups were analyzed based on Student's t test and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with a Duncan multiple-range test. Value of p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison among thermal treatment, ultrasound cavitation and hydrodynamic cavitation in the inactivation of soy milk lipoxygenase activity
ce:section.ce:para: To start with, the inactivation efficiencies of lipoxygenase activity by ultrasonic and hydrodynamic cavitation were compared with thermal treatments at 60, 70, and 80 °C (Fig. 1 ). The lipoxygenase activity in soy milk before inactivation treatment was 8280 U/mg. Thermal processing at 60 °C had negligible influence on lipoxygenase stability, whereas the lipoxygenase activity declined steadily under the exposure to 70 and 80 °C. Meanwhile, ultrasonication significantly enhanced the inactivation of lipoxygenase by traditional thermal process (Fig. 1a–c). After ultrasound treatment at 522.5 W/L for 12 min, the inactivation efficiencies of lipoxygenase were 19.5 % at 60 °C, 96.4 % at 70 °C and 100 % at 80 °C, all of which were significantly higher than individual thermal treatments (2.5 % at 60 °C, 13.2 % at 70 °C and 95.1 % at 80 °C). Therefore, it can be deduced that ultrasound caviation and temperature had synergistic effect on the inactivation of lipoxygenase. Similar results were reported in the study of Xu et al., who found that thermosonication treatment can effectively inactivated polyphenol oxidase in the mushroom extract [35].

On the other hand, the hydrodynamic cavitation treatment with the cavitation number of 0.013 for 240 min also led to the 79.3 % loss of lipoxygenase activity in soy milk (Fig. 1d). Previous research reported that HC could inactivate enzymes such as polyphenol oxidase, pectin methyl esterases and peroxidase enzymes in juices [36]. Throughout HC treatment, the temperature of soymilk was enhanced from 25 °C to 61.3 °C due to the partial transition of hydraulic energy to thermal energy. Then, the soymilk samples were incubated at 50 °C and 60 °C in a water bath separately for 240 min to verify the contribution of HC to lipoxygenase inactivation. It can be found that incubation at both 50 °C and 60 °C resulted in minor influence on soymilk lipoxygenase activiy. Lipoxygenase activity was merely declined by 18.8 % under incubation at 60 °C. Thus, it can be conformed that the decline of lipoxygenase activity under HC treatment was due to the cavitation itself, rather than the temperature rising.

The similar phenomenon of decline in lipoxygenase activity of soymilk with UC treatment has been reported by chantapakul[35], while for HC treatment few study have been reported. Both UC treatment with temperature and HC treatment can inactive lipoxygenase activity, and the results conveyed higher inactivation efficiency for UC treatment in a short time (12 min) than the HC counterpart.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature profile of ultrasound cavitation and hydrodynamic cavitation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a–c presents the temperature profiles during ultrasound treatment. The temperature was difficult to the work temperature of 70 °C using the water bath without ultrasound treatment when the sample was placed in an open system. When heat treatment (70 °C) was coupled with the acoustic energy, the working temperature of the sample was rapidly reached within 3 min, which greatly reduced the processing time. The acoustic energy also promoted an increase in the temperature of the liquid medium. The formation and subsequent collapse of cavitation bubbles in the liquid medium shocked molecules, which increased the temperature[38]. Thus, the heat supplied to samples was due to the thermal energy constantly provided by the heating water bath and acoustic cavitation.

Fig. 2d describes the temperature profiles during HC treatment. The initial temperature of the soy milk was 23 °C. There was no additional heat input throughout the process, but the temperature of the sample increased continuously with the processing time. The maximum temperature reached to 61.3 °C at 4 h, which contributed to the inactivation of lipoxygenase. The part of increase temperature in the sample may be due to the formation, growth, and subsequent collapse of cavitation bubbles formed in the liquid medium under a reduced pressure, which created extraordinary pressures of ∼ 1000 bar, and local hotspots of ∼ 5000 K [39]. The increase temperature may also be due to the energy indued by the pump, due to the friction between the pump impeller and the liquid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parametric study of ultrasonic inactivation of lipoxygenase in soy milk
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrasound coupled temperature treatment had a significant effect on the inactivation of lipoxygenase, so the influences of different operating parameters, including AED level, temperature and processing time on the inactivation of lipoxygenase were further studied. For this purpose, a four-layered feed-forward ANN model with two hidden layers was first constructed to represent the quantitative relationship between these parameters and inactivation efficiency (Fig. 3 a). Regression of predicted values obtained after data training, verification and testing is presented in Supplementary Fig. S2.

This model has an high R 2 (approaching 1) value and low RMSE (2.1 %) and ADD values (11.6 %), implying that this model was qualified to predict the changes of lipoxygenase inactivation efficiency as functions of the studied operating parameters. The weight and bias values corresponding to the neurons in the hidden and output layers are given in Supplementary Table 1.

Based on the ANN model, a 4D plot reflecting the inactivation efficiency as functions of the independent variables was generated by programming using slice function in Matlab (Fig. 3b). According to the color changes, the effects of ultrasound energy, temperature and processing time on the inactivation efficiency of lipoxygenase can be easily visualized. It can be easily observed that the lipoxygenase inactivation efficiency by ultrasound increased slowly with the input ultrasound energy entered into the system. According to the color change along with the Y-axis in Fig. 3b, it was found that the inactivation efficiency increased with the temperature. It was also observed that the ultrasound energy dissipated in the system has little effect on the inactivation efficiency of lipoxygenase at 60 °C. The range of conditions for enzyme inactivation were as follows: the temperature was set as 75 to 80 °C; the time was maintained for at least 8 min; the ultrasonic power is at least 183 W/L. Because there was a higher inactivation efficiency (96.4 %) of lipoxygenase with ultrasound treatment at 70 °C for 12 min than that with ultrasound treatment at 60 °C for 12 min (19.5 %), and 80 °C was higher temperature than 70 °C, so the further studies were conducted under the condition of 522.5 W/L at 70 °C for 12 min, considering the inactivation efficiency and low temperature.

For HC system, just processing time was investigated at same intensity of cavitation duing to defects of the equipment. Moreover, there are other parameters that can affect the inactivation efficiency, including cavitation temperature, cavitation pressure and the Venturi tube aperture [37]. For future studies, the effects of these factors need to be taken into consideration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasound and hydrodynamic cavitation on color
ce:section.ce:para: The measured pH and colors of the treated and untreated control soy milk samples are shown in Table 1 . The pH of the treatment media influences enzyme sensitivity. So, the inactivation of lipoxygenase was challenging in more acidic media [37]. Therefore, a more acidic soy milk sample would require a longer processing time. In this study, the pH of treated samples was not significantly changed by the ultrasonic and hydrodynamic cavitation process.

Color is another important index to evaluate the appearance quality of soy milk. As shown in Table 1, the heat-treated sample showed a minimal total color change (TCD). The TCD changed significantly when heat and sonication were combined. A similar trend was observed for hydrodynamic cavitation treatment. The greatest change was the L value which indicates the lightness of a sample, where soy milk samples were darker after being treated with the ultrasonic and hydrodynamic cavitation process. Previous studies have shown that ultrasound can affect color [40]. Possibly because cavitation reduced fat globules’ size, which is responsible for light reflection and for producing the white color of milk [35]. Overall, the results conclude that HC-treated samples had higher color change with a value of approximately 7.03 in comparison to UC-treated samples.

Turbiscan stability index (TSI) is a stability kinetic parameter used to monitor the scattering and transmission intensity of samples. It can comprehensively reflect the instability process of soy milk during the test process, including flocculation, coalescence, and stratification [41]. The smaller the TSI value, the more stable the measured system. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the TSI value decreased significantly after heating treatment. However, soy milk showed better stability after ultrasound treatment than that after heating treatment indicating that ultrasound improved the stability of soy milk. Fig. 4b depicted the effect of hydrodynamic cavitation processing time on the stability of soy milk. The TSI value decreased upon extending HC processing time, which demonstrated that HC processing also improved the stability of soy milk. The reason could be that soy milk of ultrasound and HC treatment had smaller particle size. The result of stability is consistent with particle size test of the soy milk.

The particle size characteristics of soy milk are important parameters for evaluating its stability. Therefore, the particle size distributions and the volume mean diameter (D4,3) of soy milk samples before and after treatment were measured. As shown in Fig. 5 b, untreated soy milk displayed a main peak around 0.201 µm. However, soy milk had a main peak around 0.201 µm as well as a shoulder peak around 4.889 µm. After UC treatment, the main peak shifted to a smaller particle size, and the area of the shoulder peak decreased. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 5a, the D4,3 value of the non-treated sample was the largest, while US treatment significantly reduced D4,3 value from 2.89 µm to 0.82 µm. The results suggested that ultrasound reduced the particle size of soy milk. Fig. 5c shows the effect of hydrodynamic cavitation treatment on the particle size of soy milk. After HC treatment, the main peak shifted to a smaller particle size, the area of the main peak increased, and the area of the shoulder peak decreased, the D4,3 value significantly reduced D4,3 value from 2.89 µm to 0.89 µm, which all suggested that HC could decreased the particle size of soy milk. Similar results have been reported in which ultrasound reduced the particle size of mung bean protein (Zhong et al., 2018), and HC reduced the particle size of soybean glycinin and raw milk[43,44]. The possible reason for ultrasound and HC were as follows. For ultrasound, while ultrasonic effectiveness might be lower at higher temperatures due to a combination of cavitation bubbles with vapor bubbles, ultrasound remained capable of breaking down heat-induced aggregates due to the acoustic cavitation, microstreaming, and turbulent forces caused by ultrasound [45]. For HC, the cavitation, microturbulence, high -intensity shear, and shock waves caused by HC have enhanced the particle sizes disruption of raw milk (Gregersen et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of treatments on the total amino acid content
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the effect of HC treatment and temperature treatment alone (70 °C) or ultrasound-assisted treatments on the total free amino acid content of soy milk. Statistical analysis indicated that total free amino acid content was significantly (P < 0.05) affected by HC and ultrasound treatment. As can be seen from Fig. 6a, the content of total free amino acids significantly (P < 0.05) increased upon increasing the HC treatment time from 2 h to 4 h. However, the total free amino acid content treated after 1 h treatment did not increase significantly compared with the control. It can be observed from Fig. 6b that after ultrasound, the total free amino acid content was not significantly affected by thermal treatment alone (70 °C). In contrast, significant differences were observed between ultrasound treatment and the control. Similar results have been reported that multiple amino acids are observed to be enhanced when exposed to the US and HC treatment [47]. According to Perez-Santaescolastica et al. [46], the ultrasound treatment can modify the protein structure due to partial cleavage of intermolecular hydrophobic interactions, rather than peptide or disulphide bonds increased the release of amino acids. It was speculated that the increase of amino acid in soy milk might due to partial cleavage of intermolecular hydrophobic interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of US and HC on the tertiary and secondary structure of protein in soy milk
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy can be used to characterize the tertiary structure of proteins, because the intrinsic fluorescence of phenylalanine (Phe), tyrosine (Tyr), and tryptophan (Trp) residues are sensitive to the polarity of their environment [11]. The endogenous fluorescence mainly came from hydrophobic Trp residues and hydrophobic Tyr residues when the excitation wavelength was 280 nm. As shown in Fig. 7 a, compared with the control, the endogenous fluorescence intensity of the soybean protein decreased significantly after heating at 70 °C. Compared with heating at 70 °C, the fluorescence intensity of the soybean protein decreased further when combined with sonication. The results showed that the soybean protein unfolded slightly at 70 °C, which exposed amino acid residues to a polar environment. ultrasound treatment accelerated this exposure, which resulted in the rapid inactivation of lipoxygenase.

As presented in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence intensity of the soybean protein decreased upon increasing the HC treatment time. The possible reason was that HC promoted the expansion of the protein structure, which increased Trp polarity. In addition, the fluorescence of the soybean protein showed a weak red- shift, which indicated that more fluorescent groups were exposed to the protein surface and that the structure of the protein unfolded. This exposed the fluorescent groups to a more polar environment and destroyed the soybean protein tertiary structure. Xu et al. observed a similar trend when investigating the effect of flat sweep frequency and pulsed ultrasound on the conformation of mushroom polyphenol oxidase [48].

The tertiary structure of a protein is formed by secondary structure interacting with each other through disulfide bonds, hydrogen bonds, and other molecular interactions (e.g., hydrophobic interactions). The destruction of the tertiary structure may change the secondary structure, which can be tracked using circular dichroism. Polypeptide chains within a protein molecule are composed of α-helixes, β-sheets, β-turns, and random coils, which reflect the changes in the secondary structures of protein [49]. Fig. 7c-d illustrates changes in the secondary structures of soybean protein after HC and ultrasound treatment.

As shown in Fig. 7d, upon increasing the HC treatment time from 0 to 4 h, the α-helixes in the secondary structures of soybean protein significantly decreased by 52.90 %, while the number of β-sheets significantly increased by 0.43-fold. The β-turn content significantly decreased, whereas the random coil content significantly increased. As shown in Fig. 7c, the α-helix content dropped from 34.1 % to 32.4 %, while the β-sheet content slightly rose from 23.2 % to 26.1 % accompanied by a significant decrease in the number of β-turn when heated at 70 °C. The random coil showed an upward trend. The same trend was observed when sonicated at 70 °C, in which the α-helix content decreased by 14.4 %, and the β-sheet content decreased by 32.8 %. This change might be attributed to the aggregate formation in amyloid[54]. And this result was similar to the findings of Chen et al. [50], and these results indicate that the decrease in lipoxygenase activity was related to the change of the secondary structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Volatile flavor compounds in soy milk
ce:section.ce:para: Changes in the composition variations of the flavor compounds of soy milk were investigated by GC–MS. As shown in Fig. 8 a-b and table 2, In total, 21 beany flavors were identified from soy milk, including 11 aldehydes, 5 alcohols, 3 ketones, and 1 furan compounds. As shown in Table 2 , the contents of hexanal (727.09 μg/L), (E)-2-hexanal (120.34 μg/L), 1-Hexanol (120.34 μg/L), (E)-2-heptenal (196.14 μg/L), and (E)-2-octenal (288.62 μg/L) were higher before treatment. The main components, hexanal and 1-hexanol are reportedly responsible for the beany flavors [51]. The contents of the above-mentioned volatile flavor compounds gradually decreased after thermosonication treatment. There was a gradual decrease upon increasing the HC treatment time. Some volatile flavor compounds (including E-2-pentenal, E-2-nonenal, E-2-decenal, nonanal, E-E-2,4-decadienal, Z-2-penten-1-ol, and furan 2-pentyl) treated for 4 h were higher than those for 2 h. As shown in Fig. 8b, the total contents of aldehydes, furans, acid, ketones, and alcohols were calculated. The total content of aldehydes was the highest, while octanoic acid was the lowest among all beany flavor compounds. Moreover, the total content of beany flavor compounds decreased after UC and HC treatment. The possible reasons were as follows. Firstly, UC and HC treatment could induce protein structural changes [42,44], which may adsorb the flavor compounds. An interesting research showed that soy protein isolate could bind the flavor compounds [52]. Secondly, it was inferred that acoustic cavitation and HC treatment inactivated the lipoxygenase activity of soy milk, which reduced the generation of beany flavor compounds. The increase in beany flavor compounds after HC treatment for 4 h, was possibly because the free radicals induced by sonication and HC might have facilitated the auto-oxidation reactions[53], resulting in an increase in beany flavor compounds.

This research explored the effect of acoustic cavitation and HC on the lipoxygenase activity, quality, and protein structure of soy milk. The application of acoustic cavitation and HC increased the inactivation efficiency of lipoxygenase. The ANN results revealed that inactivation efficiency increased with the AED level at 70 °C and 80 °C, while the effect of AED level on the inactivation process was unclear below 70 °C. Turbiscan test results showed that HC and acoustic cavitation decreased the instability index of soy milk. Both HC and acoustic cavitation treatments reduced the particle size of soy milk. The results of Turbiscan test and particle size analysis indicated that soy milk was more stable after treatment. The treatment of soy milk with HC and acoustic cavitation also affected the color parameters. For both treatments, the total free amino acid content significantly increased (P < 0.05). GC–MS results showed that the beany-flavor compounds in soy milk decreased, implying that the inactivation of lipoxygenase decreased the content of beany-flavor compounds. Furthermore, acoustic cavitation and HC changed the tertiary and secondary structure of soy milk proteins, which explains why lipoxygenase was inactivated.

Acoustic cavitation and HC is a promising technology to inactivate lipoxygenase to produce low-beany flavor soy products. Due to the limitation of ultrasound propagation and the high equipment cost, acoustic cavitation is not suitable for large-scale industrial applications. However, HC can suit scale-up operation for industrial utilization of soy milk. Future studies for HC processing of soy milk should focus on how to reduce the processing time.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.32072351) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, China (No. KYCXJC2022001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2023.106692.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of sonication on retention of anthocyanins in blackberry juice
